# Every Magazine Piece On The SF AI Scene

**From:** Astral Codex Ten <astralcodexten@substack.com>  
**Date:** 2026-05-14T05:40:29.000Z

---

View this post on the web at https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/every-magazine-piece-on-the-sf-ai

On a chilly San Francisco day, I found myself in a “group house”, a house where multiple people live in the same house. My host, a bespectacled man named Theodore Wong, grabbed a Dasani water bottle and half-eaten burrito from the refrigerator (a device used to keep food cold - according to Chang’s health-conscious bio-hacker roommate, it “prevents spoiling”). Then he started to speak.
“There is a serious risk that artificial intelligence is going to kill everyone in the world. You really need to explain this to your readers. We are desperate to get this information out. I’m begging you, please don’t make this a human interest story like all the others. We need for people to know the exact arguments,” he intoned nerdily, and proceeded to give me a twenty minute lecture including lots of words like “compute”, “exponential”, and “slaughterbot”.
This was my introduction to the wacky world of the San Francisco tech scene, where people drink Dasani water and say things about AI - a technology which, they tell me, “no really your readers need to know this, stop looking at my water bottle and listen to what I’m saying, I beg you.”
My next stop was the OpenAI office. Over a “rice bowl”, the trendy new food which AI acolytes eat to show their membership in “the ingroup”, Lamia Burgham told me about her work with the company.
Burgham, a slender willowy 28-year-old with auburn hair and an Evian water bottle, said that “Oh thank goodness a journalist is here. AI is starting to recursively self-improve. We really need for the public to start paying attention.” The stream of words emanating from her sinuous, sensuous lips was broken only by occasional sips from her Evian water bottle entering same. She punctuated her speech with an occasional sob from her sapphire-blue eyes. 
It was time to do some journalism. “Have you ever been in a ‘cuddle puddle’?”, I asked.
“What?” she said, briefly stunned by my hard-hitting Chotineresque interview style. “Look, we can’t even build new benchmarks fast enough to measure - “
“You’re evading the question,” I said, and got up and left. It was time for my next interview. Trevor MacGregor is a 23 year old college dropout working on Pennybags, an AI app to help people cheat at Monopoly. He showed me around his company’s plant-having office in the ritzy Civic Center district - which boasted a cafeteria, a printer, and a bathroom where employees could relieve themselves without ever having to leave the office. While we walked, Trevor gave me his pitch.
“So, it’s an AI-assisted app to help people cheat at Monopoly,” he said. “You point your cell phone camera at the gameboard and send it to the AI, and it tells you whether you should bid on a property or not.”
“I always bid on every property I land on,” I told him. “Is that a bad strategy?”
“Uh, I don’t know, I’ve never played. But the app can also send you an alert when it notices your opponents aren’t looking, so you can swipe money from the bank.”
“Don’t you worry that this ruins a beloved childhood game for absolutely no benefit?”
“Well, it’s like I always like to say. This technology exists. You can’t put it back in the box. Unlike Monopoly hotels, which you can put back in the box when the other players’ backs are turned.”
“Trevor, do you think readers should treat your company as a metaphor for all AI, if not all technology in general?”
“Yeah, I think that would be fair.”
“And one more question - are you personally loathsome?”
“Oh yeah, I have lots of psychological hangups and annoying quirks that discredit not only me as an individual, but the entire class of people I belong to. I’ve never kissed a girl, but also, I’m oversexed and promiscuous. My politics are the opposite of yours. I once wrote a LinkedIn article on why Ready Player One is better than Shakespeare.”
We were really making progress here. But to get the other side of the story, I had to talk to someone from “effective accelerationism” (or, as its proponents call it, “eff/a”), a totally real movement which definitely deserves to be covered in every article like this one. I met with Dayden Howe at his “group house”, a house where multiple people live in the same house. It had a big green couch, bottles of Arrowhead water in the “refrigerator”, and a digital clock - a popular tool for “efficiency-maxxers” who want to plan their day down to the minute. 
Dayden strode through the door twenty minutes after our scheduled interview time. “Sorry I’m late,” he said. “My dentist appointment ran over.”
“Is Dentist-Maxxing The Newest Fad Among Silicon Valley Elites?”
“Sorry, what?”
“Oh, sorry, I was just musing aloud about potential future articles to write. Tell me about AI.”
“AI has the potential to cure all diseases,” he told me. “Previously, this kind of research was limited to a tiny handful of geniuses. But with the ability to create intelligence on demand, we could go to space, invent new forms of power, maybe even conquer death itself.”
“Have you ever been in a ‘cuddle puddle’?”, I asked.
“Um, no,” said Dayden. “I’m just saying, AI could be a total transformation of human existence, for the better. You could have a world without work, where people could paint pictures in the morning, grow flowers in your garden in the afternoon, and play tennis in the evening.”
“Is Tennis The Latest Bay Area Tech-Bro Craze?” I asked.
“No, I was just giving it as an example. I’m saying people could do anything they wanted, instead of being yoked to the logic of capitalist production. That’s why banning AI or data centers might be such a missed opportunity. It’s why it makes me so angry when people only focus on the risks of AI, and ignore the benefits. You’re not going to do that, right? Focus your whole article around the risks?”
“Are you sure you’ve never been in a cuddle puddle?” I pressed. “Or maybe a polycule?”
“I don’t date people,” said Dayden. “It’s too thermodynamically inefficient.”
I later learned that “thermodynamic” was a piece of “tech jargon” that Silicon Valley elites say to show their “membership” in the ingroup. I wished I had more time to expose their strange yet horrible culture, but I had just gotten word that someone was discriminating against a minority in Iowa, and I needed to get there quick before another journalist beat me to the scoop.
“One final question,” I asked. “The Zizians.”
“How is that a question?” asked Dayden, evasively. It seemed like our interview was over.
As I boarded the flight to Des Moines, I thought about what I had learned. Silicon Valley is a land of Dasani water bottles - but also, sometimes, of Arrowhead water bottles. There are risks to AI, like cheating at Monopoly - but also potential benefits, like using it to finish the conclusion section of an article you’ve already lost interest in. To put it with honest bluntness, delving into AI isn’t about goblins —— it’s about ourselves.

Unsubscribe https://substack..Thqog1sxKJREqVZaAkoA0mXqEQP__OLdUpwKzhmvUYg?
